name: Conic Sections — Circle, Parabola, Ellipse, Hyperbola
whatIsIt: Slice a cone with a flat plane at different angles and you get four curves called CONIC SECTIONS: a CIRCLE (straight across), an ELLIPSE (tilted, a squashed circle), a PARABOLA (parallel to the slant side), and a HYPERBOLA (steep, two branches). Their equations: circle x² + y² = r²; parabola y² = 4ax; ellipse x²/a² + y²/b² = 1; hyperbola x²/a² − y²/b² = 1.
whyNeeded: These curves are everywhere: planets travel in ellipses, a thrown ball or a torch beam makes a parabola, satellite dishes and headlights are parabolic, and navigation and comet paths use hyperbolas. One family of shapes, endless uses.
realLifeUses: The path of a thrown ball or water fountain (parabola)
Satellite dishes and car headlights (parabolic reflectors)
A circle for wheels, plates and clock faces

vocabulary.meaning: A curve made by slicing a cone: circle, ellipse, parabola or hyperbola.
All points at distance r from the centre; r is the radius.
A single U-shaped curve, y² = 4ax; the path of a thrown object.
A squashed circle, x²/a² + y²/b² = 1; the shape of an orbit.

story.title: Slicing the Ice-Cream Cone
story.text: Sir holds a cone and slices it different ways. Straight across → a perfect circle. Tilt the knife a little → an oval ellipse. Cut parallel to the slanted side → an open parabola. Cut steeply down → a two-branch hyperbola. 'Four famous curves from one cone!' Neha marvels. Sir writes the circle's rule: x² + y² = r², so a circle x² + y² = 25 has radius 5.
story.extractedProblem: A circle has equation x² + y² = 25. What is its radius?

visual.caption: Slice a cone with a plane: level → a circle, tilted → an ellipse, steeper → a parabola, steepest → a hyperbola. Drag to orbit.

teachingGallery.title: One cone, four curves
teachingGallery.note: The slice angle decides the curve: flat → circle, tilted → ellipse, parallel to slant → parabola, steep → hyperbola.

examples.caption: A flat slice gives a circle.
A parabola — the path of a thrown ball and the shape of a dish.
An ellipse — a squashed circle, the shape of a planet's orbit.

standardMethod.summary: Identify the conic from its equation, then read its key number. Circle x² + y² = r² → radius √(r²). Match real objects: orbits = ellipse, thrown ball / dish = parabola.
steps: Look at the equation's form to name the conic.
Circle: x² + y² = r² → radius = √(the right-hand number).
Parabola y² = 4ax: one U-shaped branch (dishes, thrown objects).
Ellipse x²/a² + y²/b² = 1: a closed oval (orbits).
Hyperbola x²/a² − y²/b² = 1: two open branches (the minus sign is the giveaway).

workedExamples.problem: Find the radius of the circle x² + y² = 49.
How many types of conic section are there?
steps: Slice a cone at different angles.

commonMistakes.mistake: Saying the radius of x² + y² = 25 is 25 instead of 5.
commonMistakes.fix: The right side is r² (the radius SQUARED). Take the square root: r = √25 = 5.

commonMistakes.mistake: Confusing an ellipse (plus) with a hyperbola (minus).
commonMistakes.fix: x²/a² + y²/b² = 1 is an ELLIPSE (plus, one closed loop). The MINUS version is a hyperbola (two branches).

easy.q: How many types of conic section are there?
Circle x² + y² = 25. What is the radius?

medium.q: A satellite dish / torch reflector has the shape of a…
hintLadder: It focuses signals to one point.

challenge.q: For x² + y² = 25 a student says the radius is 25. What's the fix?

options.label: The right side is r²; take √25 = 5

challenge.answer: The right side is r²; take √25 = 5

questions.q: A planet's orbit is shaped like a…
The equation with a MINUS (x²/a² − y²/b² = 1) is a…

teachBackPrompt: Slice a cone (or a cone of clay) different ways for a grown-up to show a circle, ellipse, parabola and hyperbola. Then show that x² + y² = 25 is a circle of radius 5 (√25), not 25.

revisionCard.summary: Conic sections = curves from slicing a cone: circle (x²+y²=r², radius √RHS), ellipse (x²/a²+y²/b²=1, orbit), parabola (y²=4ax, thrown ball/dish), hyperbola (x²/a²−y²/b²=1, minus sign, two branches). Radius is √(right-hand side), not the right-hand side.
